data.title: Micron: The Peak Memory Myth
data.description:
Fears of peak memory are overblown in our opinion, and one victim since reporting its stellar earnings just a few weeks ago has been Micron Technology, Inc. (MU). Make no mistake at all that Micron’s earnings power has simply been incredible, and the growth so rapid, the market is questioning whether it is sustainable.

Despite management’s positive outlook and the consensus views from analysts on the growth, Micron still has a muted multiple relative to many other names in the AI revolution. In fact, the stock rallied tremendously off of a rare public Strong Buy call we made on Micron in late 2025, but has since given all those wonderful gains back.

As members of BAD BEAT Investing know, we take some of those profits when we are up over 25% in a short-time, but do like to let a house position run. But the reset has been painful.

With that said, Micron has been a very popular ticker in our chatrooms, and we have provided a number of thoughts. The day shares dipped back to about $320 we noted the selloff as incredible, and that it was near the top of our list to buy once again.

While we have bounced mildly off of that recent take, we still think these levels are very buyable, and do not buy into the peak memory thesis. In this column we discuss reasons for the selloff and note the strong performance. In our opinion this has been caused by many people taking Alphabet/Google (GOOG) at their word that less memory will be needed due to TurboQuant, and fears that higher capex out of Micron will not bear fruit. Let us discuss.

The thesis here is simple for Micron and that is its position in the broader AI stack. The company sells memory and storage solutions that are used all over the place. Not only in data center cloud servers, but also in many devices for office, personal use, cars and trucks, etc. With AI being utilized more and more the amount of memory needed to run inquiries and models has surged given the complexity of the input requests and the amount of calculation that goes into formulating responses.

This is where the recent news out of Google weighed a bit. In case you missed it, Google recently unveiled a technology called TurboQuant, designed to drastically compress the key-value cache memory necessary for large language model inference. It would seem many are simply taking the company at its word that this will reduce global need for memory, perhaps as competitors adopt similar technology. At the most basic level Google assets this method can reduce the memory footprint by at least 6x without sacrificing model accuracy, which could theoretically cut the demand for memory in AI operations. It did not take long for memory makers like Micron and its competitors to see their stocks fall further following the announcement.

There are a few reasons we do not buy into peak memory, despite this seemingly bearish news out of Google. First, if it can reduce memory demand, would that not lead to pushing the envelope and users employing the highest intensity computing demands it could think of? That is, while reducing current need, it could drive even more intense demand, actually increasing memory demand.

Second, right now, demand and orders still remain high. Demand is not going to dry up overnight. Memory makers are still currently prioritizing high-margin high bandwidth memory for data centers. That will not change for at least a few quarters and by doing so it has caused a hit to the supply of components for consumer electronics like PCs and phones, which has driven prices higher. While there has been some moderation very recently in memory prices, it remains very elevated.

Third, the demand for memory in data centers has also strained manufacturing capacity, meaning there is a large backlog of orders to be filled.

Now, not many are talking about how the agentic AI models are often trial and error. That is our fourth point, a lot of these fail, meaning back to the drawing board to revamp and retool. While the models in a general sense are so much better than just two years ago, this constant failure and refinement keeps demand high. In an effort to be balanced, recognizing the high failure rate, it could lead to companies giving up on those efforts, hurting demand. But as of right now, we cannot see the space being near peak memory demand. As an aside, it reminds us of the peak oil fears of the 1990s and early 2000s.

While that is the broader view, Micron itself saw investor concern over its announcement on capex. To us, it was a bit of a weak excuse to sell the stock, but rapid-returns were made and investors were locking in gains, triggering more selling. The fear again being that if all this money is being spent, and we are near a peak memory event, then the return on investment would be poor. While this is a potentially real and fundamental issue for the company, the market is probably overstating its concern near-term, shooting first for the sell button to ask questions later.

What is the major concern? Well Micron boosted its 2026 capital spending plan by $5 billion, or about 25% to bring its 2026 capital investments to over $25 billion. However, we think this spending announcement was less of this issue. While spending concerns for the aforementioned reasons were evident, what really gave investor’s anxiety in our opinion was that management had also indicated that its capital spending would rise again in 2027. So this spending caused fear of too much supply. However, as we assert, there is simply no sign that too much supply is on the horizon.

With that said what investors may be missing is that Micron and related names dealing with memory have always been cyclical. But they broke that cyclicality with unprecedented demand, almost becoming secular. That is, enjoying a constant stream of demand. The recent trading suggests the market is treating Micron and the competition like it will be going back to being cyclical. That all remains to be seen, but the data right now strongly suggests that memory supply needs a lot more time to truly catch up to demand.

And so, with an incredible reset in shares, with expectations for this fiscal year to see nearly 600% EPS growth from last fiscal year, and fiscal 2027 to see 70% EPS growth, shares are absurdly cheap right now. If EPS expansion dries up in fiscal 2028, which is many quarters away, it would still be cheap. But Fiscal 2028 does not start for over 18 months. Typically, the market would not front load a lack of growth this soon into share prices, especially with two more quarters remaining in fiscal 2026, and a stock now trading at 6.3X the consensus.

We believe MU stock has overshot to the downside in this correction, especially the closer it gets to $300. Shares are a buy in our opinion once again.

data.title: Risk Off After Trump's Speech - Intraday Outlook On S&P 500, Nasdaq 100, AUD/USD, Gold, And WTI Crude Oil
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

US President Trump’s evening prime-time speech on Wednesday, 1 April at 9.00 p.m. Washington time does not offer any new information to global financial markets. A quick recap: Trump highlighted earlier on Tuesday, March 31, that the US-Iran war will “end soon” within the next two to three weeks.

Interestingly, Trump has indirectly issued a more “hawkish rhetoric” towards the Iranian leadership in his prime-time national address, stating that the US will hit Iran “extremely hard” in the next two to three weeks, and added that the US will also strike Iran’s electric plants if there is no deal.

Overall, there is no clear indication of a forthcoming “de-escalation” from Trump, which in turn brings the stagflation risk narrative back to the forefront again.

How do the markets react? Risk-off mode at this time of writing.

  • WTI crude oil +5.8% at around $104 per barrel
  • Brent crude oil +6.4% at around $106 per barrel
  • US Dollar Index +0.4%
  • AUD/USD -0.6%
  • Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) -1.7%
  • S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 E-mini futures -1.1% and -1.3%
  • Japan’s Nikkei 225 -2.2%
  • Hong Kong’s Hang Seng Index -1.1%
  • China’s CSI 300 -0.7%
  • South Korea’s KOSPI 200 -4.7%
  • Singapore’s Straits Times Index -0.7%

We have highlighted in our earlier detailed report that the prior three-day rebound seen on the three major benchmark US stock indices, S&P 500, Nasdaq 100, and Dow Jones Industrial Average are likely a mean reversion rebound sequence (dead cat bounces) within their respective medium-term downtrend phases.

Let’s focus now on the short-term trajectory (1-3 days) on the S&P 500, Nasdaq 100, AUD/USD, gold (XAU/USD), and WTI crude oil from a technical analysis perspective.

S&P 500 - Bearish reaction at descending channel resistance with bearish momentum

The price actions of the US S&P 500 CFD index (a proxy of the S&P 500 E-mini futures) have staged a bearish reaction right after a retest on its 20-day moving average and upper boundary of a descending channel in place since February 26, 2026 high (see Fig. 1).

In addition, the hourly RSI momentum indicator has staged a bearish breakdown below its former ascending trendline support after it flashed a bearish divergence condition at its overbought zone.

Watch the 6,600 key short-term pivotal resistance, and a break below 6,455 near-term support is likely to trigger another potential bearish impulsive down-move sequence to expose the next intermediate supports at 6,327/6,290 and 6,218/6,210.

However, a clearance above 6,600 invalidates the near-term bearish scenario for an extension of the mean reversion rebound towards the 6,648/6,730 zone.

Nasdaq 100 - Bearish reaction below 20-day moving average with bearish momentum

The price actions of the US Nasdaq 100 CFD index (a proxy of the Nasdaq 100 E-mini futures) have staged a bearish reaction below its downward-sloping 20-day moving average, coupled with the hourly RSI momentum indicator’s bearish breakdown below its key ascending trendline support (see Fig. 2).

Watch the 24,047/24,177 key short-term pivotal resistance, and a break below 23,400 near-term support is likely to trigger another potential bearish impulsive down move sequence to expose the next intermediate supports at 23,085 and 22,800/22,680.

On the other hand, a clearance above 24,177 invalidates the near-term bearish scenario for an extension of the mean reversion rebound towards the 24,355/24,537 zone.

AUD/USD – En route towards the lower limit of the descending channel

The minor corrective mean reversion rebound seen on the AUD/USD from its swing low of 0.6838 printed on Monday, March 30, 2026, is likely to be over (see Fig. 3).

Watch the 0.6910/0.6938 key short-term pivotal resistance, and a break below 0.6838 is likely to trigger a potential fresh bearish impulsive down move sequence to expose the next intermediate supports at 0.6780/0.6760 and 0.6710 (also close to the key 200-day moving average).

However, a clearance above 0.6938 invalidates the near-term bearish scenario for an extension of the mean reversion rebound towards the next intermediate resistance at 0.7000/0.7015 (also the 50-day moving average).

Gold (XAU/USD) - At risk of a “bearish flag” breakdown below $4,775 key resistance

The recent seven-day rally (high to low) of 17% from the March 23, 2026 low seen in gold is likely to be a corrective mean reversion rebound in the form of a “bearish flag” within its medium-term downtrend phase (see Fig. 4).

The bullish momentum of the 7-day rally is getting exhausted. The hourly RSI momentum indicator has just staged a bearish breakdown below its key ascending trendline support after a prior bearish divergence condition at its overbought zone

Watch the $4,775 key medium-term pivotal resistance, and a break below $4,524/4,486 support (the lower boundary of the “bearish flag”) is likely to trigger another potential minor bearish impulsive down move sequence in the first step to expose the next intermediate supports at $4,319 and $4,167/4,099 (also the key 200-day moving average).

On the flip side, a clearance above $4,775 invalidates the near-term bearish scenario for an extension of the mean reversion rebound towards the next intermediate resistance at $4,980/5,039 (also the 50-day moving average).

WTI crude oil - Bulls find support above 20-day moving average, traded back above $102.25

The intraday sell-off seen on Wednesday, April 1, 2026, for West Texas oil CFD (a proxy of the WTI crude oil futures) has been erased as its price actions trade back above $102.25 (see Fig. 5).

Watch the $96.44/93.70 key short-term pivotal support (close to the 20-day moving average) to maintain a potential fresh minor impulsive up move sequence towards $116.56/119.54 in the first step within its medium-term uptrend phase.

A clearance above $116.56/119.54 opens scope for the next intermediate resistance to come in at $124.40, followed by the major resistance of $131.30/132.67 seen during the onset of the Russia-Ukraine war.

On the other hand, a break below $93.70 negates the bullish tone for a slide towards the medium-term pivotal support zone of $88.36/86.50.

Original Post

data.title: American Financial: Margin Pressure Limits Upside
data.description:
Shares of American Financial Group (AFG) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about 3% of their value. While AFG has a history of strong underwriting, results have been more mixed over the past year, which has pressured shares, especially given broader fears of peak profitability in the industry. Beyond this, its exposure to apartment rentals within its investment portfolio has been a headwind, though its lack of private credit exposure is an important differentiator. I last covered shares in November, rating the stock a “Hold,” and since then they have lost about 2%, in line with the market’s 3% decline.

Now, the insurance sector is a large investor in private credit, which has been a headwind for performance given growing fears of a default cycle. However, this is less of a concern for AFG, which maintains a conservative fixed income portfolio. Within its $17 billion investment portfolio, 65% sits in fixed income, and 96% of that portfolio is investment grade. The portfolio is also relatively short-dated with a 2.9 year maturity and a 5.11% yield. I expect the portfolio yield to finish this year within 5bps of current levels.

That said, it has some exposures to be mindful of, notably $1.2 billion of CLOs (collateralized loan obligations), which are vehicles supported by a pool of leveraged loans. However, essentially the entire portfolio is rated AAA, and the AAA tranche of a CLO has never taken a loss, not even in 2008-2009. I do not expect this to change, and I see little risk here. While there has been concern about excessive direct lending to software companies, just 1.5% of the portfolio, or $249 million, is loans to the entire technology sector, only a subset of which would be software.

AFG simply does not have that much exposure to the portions of the credit market that have sparked so much fear. That is because it has historically taken its investment risk elsewhere. Somewhat uniquely, AFG owns apartment buildings. Within its alternatives bucket, about 45% is dedicated to owning multifamily properties. It has $1.25 billion of properties, which have a 91% occupancy rate (a bit lower than the national average).

This allocation has been challenging in its own right because rents are falling nationally. Rent performance has disappointed me. I previously hoped for a bottom in 2025 and a return to growth. I now do not expect growth until 2027, especially as AFG’s portfolio has outsized exposure to Florida (29%), which faces significant supply. While lower construction levels should eventually support a rebound in rents, we are unlikely to see that in 2026, which will weigh on AFG’s alternative investment income performance. That said, I view the allocation favorably over the medium term, but weak household formation and strained affordability are near-term pressures. I do not expect a deterioration in 2026 relative to 2025. Still, it is a near-term headwind.

Turning to underwriting, in the company’s fourth quarter, American Financial Group earned $3.65 per share, which beat expectations by $0.34 even as revenue declined 4% to $2.1 billion. For the full year, the company earned $10.29, better than the $9.75-$9.90 I was looking for, thanks to a nice improvement in underwriting results in Q4 following mixed results earlier in the year. Net written premiums were down 1% from last year as the more competitive pricing environment has made AFG more selective in the policies it writes to preserve margin.

It reported an 84.1% combined ratio in Q4, thanks to excellent results in its crop unit. Additionally, AFG benefitted from a quiet hurricane season with just 2% of cat losses. Still, even with a solid Q4, AFG reported a 0.4% increase in its combined ratio to 91.3% in 2025. This is the 4th straight year its combined ratio has risen, and its outperformance vs. peers of 4.5% is the lowest in over a decade. Now, its 2021-2022 performance was not sustainable, as that level of margin would get competed away, and I view 90%-92.5% as a longer-run norm for the business, with 2026 likely towards the high end of this range.

During the earnings call, management noted that it is seeing softening rates as competition has picked up. Given some ongoing cost inflation, especially in heavily litigated areas (like California workers’ comp), any weakening in pricing power creates risk that underwriting margins could narrow. As you can see below, pricing power surged around COVID, given the rise in overall inflation. Since then, pricing has gradually slowed.

Rate increases are now running around 4%. This moderation in pricing inflation has driven concerns that margins may be peaking. However, price increases are still running above the overall level of inflation. I believe we have seen peak margins for the industry (holding cat activity flat), but given the fact there is still healthy premium price inflation, I believe the pace of margin compression should be modest. In other words, underwriting fundamentals are healthy, just not quite as healthy as 12 months ago.

Turning to its balance sheet, debt to capital is 28%, and the company has no maturities before 2030. This is a solid position, but I would not want to see leverage any higher than this. I expect all capital returns to be funded out of earnings. Shares offer a regular dividend yield of about 2.8%. Beyond its regular dividend, the company has a history of making special dividend payments with its excess capital. During calendar year 2025, it paid $4 per share in special dividends, and it made a $1.50 payment in February of this year already.

In 2026, management is targeting 3%-5% premium growth and a 92.5% combined ratio. Assuming an 8% return on its alternatives portfolio, it expects about $11 of EPS. Back in November, I saw the company earning no more than $10.50 in 2026. At that time, consensus was $11.52, and it has drifted lower since then, now standing at $11.09. Given the pressures on rental rates, I expect slightly weaker alternative income while I am also cautious about the pace of premium growth. As a result, I expect EPS to be in the $10.50-$11.00 range this year.

That leaves shares at ~11.8x earnings, just a bit below my fair value of 12x, or $129. I expect at least $2 of further special dividends this year on top of its regular dividend, pointing to a total return in the mid-to-upper single digits. As such, I view shares as a “Hold.” With moderate total return opportunity, they are not a "Sell." However, I do not see AFG returning to $140, as multiple expansion at a time of thinning margins and fears about sectoral investment outcomes are likely to be difficult. AFG is a relatively safe name given its lack of private credit exposure, but I would look elsewhere for greater upside opportunities.

data.title: If Iran War Ends, Korea Is A Screaming Buy
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Just a few weeks ago, I highlighted how the Korean Index (KOSPI) (EWY) had crashed 20% in two sessions, driven by an energy shock and a leverage unwind.

But with the war potentially nearing an end, could this now be the time to buy?

President Trump is looking to exit the Iran conflict, and Iran may want out, too.

As a result, oil has pulled back, volatility has eased, and risk assets are stabilizing.

Because if this really is de-escalation, then Korea may have just gone from the epicenter of risk to one of the most asymmetric opportunities in global markets.

But if that’s not the case; then the downside isn’t finished.

Is The War Really Over?

President Trump is giving the market what it wants: a swift de-escalation from the Iran war.

But it takes two parties to stop a war, and as I also highlighted last week, markets have become increasingly skeptical of Trump.

For now, oil has come off its highs and equities are bouncing, but this could still be a bear market rally.

The key issue here is that despite what the U.S. might say, there’s been no regime change in Iran, and that could be an issue.

But most of all, even if the Iran war ends soon, the Strait of Hormuz has not been secured. This means oil prices could continue to be an issue.

What we have right now is a temporary de-escalation.

But, as far as the flow of oil and gas is concerned, it does seem like the worst could now be behind us, which is great news for Korea.

Why Korea Could Be A Screaming Buy

In my last piece, I laid out how the sell-off in Korea was driven by two things: energy exposure and leverage.

Both of those have now partially reversed.

Oil is down from the panic highs, which immediately relieves some of the pressure on margins, currency, and sentiment.

More importantly, the leverage is gone, and what was once a headwind could now be a tailwind.

Just yesterday, Korea actually had to halt program trading, which is known as a sidecar halt, as volatility increased and the index rallied over 5%.

The speculation to the long side is gone, replaced by longer-term holders, and also short speculators, which could even lead to a further short squeeze.

But even more important is the AI Capex cycle.

Companies like Samsung Electronics and SK hynix sit at the very core of the AI supply chain.

The AI narrative has taken a hit in recent weeks, with memory prices taking a breather. But if we actually look at GPUs, prices are increasing across the board.

With the war potentially ending, the odds of a recession are now much lower. Korea should continue to lead the upside as the AI Capex cycle unfolds.

Technical Analysis

From a technical standpoint, we don't have a confirmed uptrend, but perhaps early signs of a reversal.

On the 4h chart, we actually bounced beautifully off the 200 EMA, and are now getting ready to break through the other key EMAs. On top of that, we have a confirmed bullish crossover in the MACD.

On the daily chart, we still observe a good reversal, but it’s true that we don’t have that much support below us.

If we fail to break to the upside now, then a lower low becomes very likely.

Risks

The biggest risk is, of course, that the war is not yet over.

This would be an issue for Korea, which is dependent on energy exported from the Middle East to support its manufacturing infrastructure.

If tensions re-escalate and energy prices move higher again, everything we just discussed reverses.

Margins would get squeezed, the currency might weaken, and equities would continue lower.

The other potential risk factor I see is that this disruption might have been enough to destabilize the global economy.

Even if this ends soon, the higher oil prices and effects on inflation could force tighter monetary policy, and this could eventually trigger an actual recession.

I don’t see evidence of this right now, but the next couple of months will be crucial in determining the final outcome of this conflict.

Final Thoughts

Korea was one of the hottest markets in 2025, with good reasons. However, speculation had gotten out of hand, and technicals were overstretched.

I see this as potentially a healthy pullback and a chance to gain exposure to an economy that is vital to the AI cycle.

Korea offers good exposure to manufacturing giants and some much-needed diversification away from the dollar.

data.title: Geely Auto: Downgrading After An Exceptional Rally Post-FY25 Results
data.description:
Update: Downgrade to "Hold"

Geely Auto (GELYF) was one of the stocks I liked earlier due to momentum from its new product line, Galaxy in late 2024. That and the Lynk&Co have done much of the heavy lifting in achieving over 3 million units sold in 2025 and revenue reaching CNY345 billion, up 44%. Profitability was also robust amid upped R&D spendings.

The market reacted extremely positively after the announcement of FY25 results in mid-March after months of decline due to policy headwinds where the market was reading into the implications of "anti-involution" and pulled subsidies in late 2025. Amid all this, Geely was one of the resilient players in the entire industry. Its H-share had surged over ~HKD22 vs. the HKD15 low in the past 6 months. The extreme movement does not reflect fundamentals purely in my opinion, and the market could have been a little over-excited after the year-end results.

Geely's resilience vs. peers

2025 results were on-par with my expectations back in September. The positive surprises were resilient gross margin (16.6% vs. 15.9% in 2024) amid a bull year for commodities, and the lack of "anti-involution" impact since early-to-mid 2025. Regulators have been advocating for automakers to shorten the time to pay suppliers. This year, none of the automakers have shown any progress on this front, the same goes for Geely. Given that a core assumption from my earlier thesis was that by shortening the payment duration would squeeze working capital, and therefore reduce short-term cash flows, this becomes a lingering risk.

My previous target price was HKD24 per share, that incorporates a strong growth expectations in 2025. As we move beyond, growth will normalize amid policy cool-down on the subsidy front. International markets will be leveraged to fill some of the void. All-in-all, I think after aggressive rally post Q4 earnings, the current HKD22-ish level offers little upside. As a result, I am downgrading the stock to a "hold".

Top-down growth outlook

Geely had delivered 44% sales growth in 2025, exceeding the 9.4% increase in sales industry wide. Meanwhile, the experience varied across automakers with headline cases by firms like BYD and Li Auto, each reflecting the struggles of their own segments. For BYD, its sheer size presents a burden that required aggressive discounts. Amid the anti-involution campaign, such actions reflecting a price were frowned upon. Therefore, sales took a hit. If we move to smaller auto competitors, their experiences have also been similar, each trying to play different product differentiation games to gain some tailwind.

Growth in 2024 and 2025 were overall robust. Auto sales were the initial "card" that policymakers played to boost demand in the economy. That tailwind is slowly fading in the background as subsidies coverage are narrowing down to trade-in purchases vs. the earlier broad base subsidy programs for EVs. Moving forward, Geely is also expecting less aggressive growth with the international markets emerging as the increasingly important driver.

Valuation

Part of the reason I liked Geely earlier was its valuation, trading at a discount to most of its peers earlier. After the release of strong 2025 results, the market went on rallying the stock confidently. I would like to caution on that given the pull-back on policy support that have caused some repricing since mid-2025. Historically, a useful analogy is Geely's peer, GW Motor (GWLLF). GWM had released strong results for 1H25. It was a similar case to Geely considering new product roll-outs and cheap valuation relative to peers with strong growth. That said, its performance after release has been sluggish given the doubts of growth sustainability in a highly competitive industry.

Using the FCFF method while accounting for the change in growth expectations, my target remains relatively unchanged vs. the earlier HKD24 per share and expects more of a downward adjustment to a range of HKD20-24. This is to account for the change of the top-down environment with lower expectations of growth. In addition, automobile companies in China have still yet to change their supplier payment practices significantly, extending the pressure on working capital.

FCFF

More on fundamentals

Overall, I think Geely's strategy and execution is worth noting. 2024 had seen tremendous breakthroughs in brands like Galaxy, Zeekr, and Lynk&Co. In 2025, sales across all lines have been resilient while noting that there was very little evidence of cannibalization while all three brands were taking a shot at higher-end markets with higher ASPs. In 2026, unit sales across the brands stabilized with very little growth - still an outperformance relative to peers.

As mentioned earlier, margins were robust in 2025 despite upped spending on research by CNY7 billion vs. the earlier fiscal year. Significant progress have been made in areas like its EV architecture, emerging solid-state battery testing, and smart driving. They puts Geely competitively in both domestic and international markets. That said, this progress should be viewed more as "sustenance" rather than breakthroughs of growth.

Sales

Outlook

Geely's performance has been extremely strong in 2025. Amid the strengths and resilience, its stock price had surged significantly that effectively offset the earlier losses due to the industry-wide fear of slow down. I think that the north of 35% rally since mid-march was really unexpected and is an opportunity to trim exposure. Slower growth is expected moving forward. Over the past 6 months, much evidence has surfaced to show how competitive the automotive industry in China really is. With lingering inventory pressure and policy threats on the "supplier payment" front, it is best to adopt a relatively cautious approach.

Overall, Geely's exports initiatives are seemed as a positive that will contribute to considerable growth in 2026 and 2027. The automotive industry in China has a significant comparative advantage globally. On this front, peers like Geely and BYD have demonstrated considerable momentum in 2025. That said, the market has arguably priced in these growth expectations already with limited upside. I am downgrading the stock to a "hold". This rating reiterates the competitiveness of the industry and the actual extent of subsidy dependence that were revealed recently, where sales plummeted for certain brands and certain models.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Eagle Bancorp Is Improving, But It's Not Enough For An Upgrade
data.description:
Sometimes, the market can behave in an irrational manner. And sometimes, that irrationality can last for weeks, months, or even years. One company that I believe the market has, since I last wrote about it in July of 2025, acted irrationally about is none other than Eagle Bancorp (EGBN). This bank, which has a market capitalization today of $743 million, has seen its share price jump 22% since I reaffirmed it as a "Sell" candidate back then. Over that period, the S&P 500 is up 3%. As disappointing as this is, at least the stock is underperforming since I originally rated it a "Sell" in October 2024. From that time through today, shares are up only 7.4%. But the market is up 12.5%. Seeing as how I consider a "Sell" candidate to be one that should underperform the market for the foreseeable future, I would say that I am turning out so far to be right about the business.

Given the surge in pricing that we have seen, you might think that there is some justification for it. But honestly, I don't really see much of a reason to be optimistic. Yes, shares are incredibly cheap on both a price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book basis. But at a time when many other banks are enjoying improvements in net interest margin, it has seen a decline. Overall profitability has taken a hit, and asset quality remains abysmal. Because of this, I cannot in good faith upgrade it at this time.

Eagle Bancorp is a mess

I think the first place that I would like to start when it comes to analyzing Eagle Bancorp today would be its income statement. At the top, we have net interest income, which in the final quarter of 2025 was just $52.6 million. That was actually down from the $60.3 million that the institution reported a year earlier. Some of this was because of about a $1.8 million change in the company's favor when it came to its provision for credit losses. But overwhelmingly, it was because of other factors, most notably changes in the composition of its balance sheet and a decline in net interest margin from 2.43% to 2.38%. For 2025 as a whole, the picture was significantly worse. Net interest income actually plummeted from $224.5 million to negative $24.8 million. This was even though, for that time, net interest margin remained unchanged at 2.37%. Instead, the company had to contend with a $293.1 million provision for credit losses. Management attributed that drop specifically to the replenishment of the reserve following net charge-offs, as well as an increase in reserves associated with commercial real estate office loans.

The office angle of this is particularly interesting to me. Management has, over the last year now, reduced its overall exposure to office properties. They have gone from representing 11% of the company's loan portfolio to representing just 8% in the course of a year. Changes in how and where people work, not to mention other factors, have led to rising vacancy rates throughout the United States. In Washington, D.C., where the company has a significant presence, the office vacancy rate rose from 19% last year to 19.5% this year. This is worse than the 17.6% office vacancy rate for the nation as a whole. So it doesn't surprise me, at least, to see that the company would face some issues on that front.

There have been some improvements that the company saw when it came to non-interest income. But because of how significant the change in net interest income has been, that improvement has not been enough to prevent net profits for the institution from falling. They declined from $15.3 million in the final quarter of 2024 to $7.6 million at the same time in 2025. And for 2025 in its entirety, the $138.1 million that the company lost was far worse than the $47 million that they lost a year earlier.

When it comes to the balance sheet, we have seen some changes as well. Management reported $9.13 billion worth of deposits at the end of 2024. Last year, that number did remain unchanged at $9.13 billion. But the good news is that while broker deposits are still drastically higher than I would like them to be, the $3.3 billion that the institution had associated with these types of deposits ended up being well below the $4 billion that it had a year earlier. This shows at least some progress has been made, with the decline in brokered deposits implying a similarly large increase in organic deposits.

Loans have historically made up a large portion of the company's overall balance sheet. They totaled $7.12 billion in 2025. But that's actually down quite a bit from the $7.82 billion that the company ended 2024 with. The greatest exposure that the institution has here would be income-producing commercial real estate that was in the amount of $3.35 billion. But the firm also had about $1.60 billion of owner-occupied commercial real estate as well as $1.34 billion of other commercial debt. Securities have also been an area where the company has declined in size. These went from $2.21 billion in 2024 to $1.83 billion in 2025. In fact, the only metric that noticeably improved that is noteworthy is cash and cash equivalents. It rose from $633.5 million to $695.7 million. Debt, meanwhile, has plummeted, going from $599.3 million in 2024 to only $76.4 million in 2025.

When you add all of these changes together, what you get is an institution that is trying to move toward lower risk. The reduction in debt is great to see, but it did require management to shrink the size of the loans and securities on its books. The bolstering of its cash position is also a sign that management has taken something of a defensive measure. This, combined with management's success in growing organic deposits while broker deposits declined, is likely what has caused a lot of optimism that has pushed the share price higher. But I would argue this is premature.

In the chart above, you can see certain asset quality metrics of the institution. Return on assets had a reading of just 0.25%. That's well below the 1% minimum that I typically look for, and it puts the company lower than any of the other five banks that I compared it to in that chart. In the chart below, meanwhile, you can see its return on equity rating of 2.63%. I typically like this reading to be 10% or more. Even though Eagle Bancorp is not the worst of the five competitors in this regard, it's pretty close.

It is true that management is working to make some changes here. The most notable change can be seen by looking at certain credit quality metrics. Although the allowance for credit losses did jump year over year, the firm went from having just 1.90% of its assets and loans that were 90 or more days past due to having just 1.04%. Meanwhile, non-performing loans dropped from 2.63% of total loans to 1.47%. So this does indicate that progress is real. This also makes sense when you consider that management is pushing for a net interest margin this year between 2.6% and 2.8%, thanks to the changes that it is making to the composition of its balance sheet. But unless we see some real tangible results from that, I think a more cautious approach is appropriate. This is especially true considering that, because of the net losses the company exhibited in 2025, you can't realistically value it in relation to earnings right now. You can, however, compare it to other similar firms on a price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book basis. This is the one area where the company shines. It is trading drastically below both book value and tangible book value. Plus, it is cheaper than all five firms that I compared it to. However, this is common for banks that are struggling.

Takeaway

The current data does show some early signs of a turnaround for Eagle Bancorp. However, I am not convinced that the data is strong enough yet to justify a bullish or even neutral stance. The company does have a couple of things going for it. But asset quality remains abysmal, and changes in the balance sheet have not yet shown up in the form of improved profitability. Until such time that we see some more compelling results, I will stick with my bearish rating on the institution.

data.title: Fixed-Income Outlook: From Oil Shock To Oil Spillover?
data.description:
Oil’s rise could linger. Here are six ways bond investors can build resilience.

Geopolitics rarely stay contained to the headlines for long. Conflict in the Middle East is already reverberating through energy markets, reminding investors how quickly war can ripple into the global economy. Oil prices are the key transmission channel, and how far they rise and how long they remain elevated could shape the outlook for growth, inflation and policy, with implications for bond markets.

War's Transmission Pipeline: Oil

The war in Iran is the latest in a series of shocks with the potential to disrupt economies and financial markets. If the conflict resolves quickly and energy prices retreat, the shock may prove short-lived. If not, it could be much more severe. The only certainty now is uncertainty.

West Texas Intermediate crude traded around $67 a barrel on February 27, the day before hostilities escalated. Within 10 days, prices had surged past $100. But what matters most for the global economy is persistence. Higher fuel costs force governments, households and businesses to spend more on energy and less elsewhere - raising the risk of a stagflationary mix of slower growth and higher prices.

For central banks, that combination is tricky. Should central bankers raise rates to rein in rising prices? Or lower rates to stem slowing growth? The quandary suggests that the Federal Reserve and European Central Bank may stay on hold as they await further clarity. And while China has accumulated an oil stockpile that should see it through the supply disruption for now, other Asian economies are major importers of oil products, and most don’t have the ability to replace oil and gas that typically come from the Middle East.

Is This 2022 All Over Again?

Today’s oil shock invites comparisons to 2022, when Russia’s invasion of Ukraine triggered a sharp surge in energy prices, reigniting inflation and forcing central banks to tighten aggressively. The result was the worst year on record for modern bond markets, which tumbled in tandem with equities. But the backdrop today is different.

Policy rates are no longer near zero, there is no post-pandemic reopening tailwind, and fiscal capacity is more constrained. That leaves central banks facing a more difficult trade-off: tightening into a supply-driven shock risks exacerbating an already fragile macro environment and increasing the risk of financial stress, while holding back allows inflation pressures to build.

This is not 2022 - and, in our view, reacting as if it were would be a mistake. So far, financial markets have been volatile but well-behaved. But geopolitical conflicts are complex and unpredictable. For bond markets, the result is greater uncertainty around the path of inflation and interest rates - conditions that call for caution but that also widen dispersion across regions and sectors, creating opportunities for active investors.

Six Strategies to Put into Action

Our outlook entering the year favored resilient portfolios - active duration, global diversification, and a balance of rate and credit risks. In our view, these strategies remain well-suited to today’s increasing uncertainty, as they help position active investors to absorb volatility and capture new opportunities as they arise.

Diversify duration. In our view, today’s environment argues for keeping bonds anchored within overall portfolios - and that means holding duration. But don’t just set your portfolio duration and forget it. When yields are higher (and bond prices lower), lengthen the duration; when yields are lower (and prices higher), trim your sails. And remember, high current yields provide a cushion against price declines.

We believe that duration should also be sourced from diverse regions. A globally diversified approach to duration may offer a sturdier foundation for bond portfolios. Curve positioning, too, is a lever that shapes how portfolios respond as the rate environment evolves.

Government bonds remain the purest source of interest rate sensitivity and remain essential for liquidity. But investors can also take duration through securitized markets such as agency mortgage-backed securities, which provide both duration and incremental yield. Securitized assets are also somewhat less exposed to energy-related disruption, in our analysis.

Adopt a balanced stance. As we see it, a balanced posture across rates and credit provides a sturdier mix of resilience and income. Among the most effective strategies are those that pair government bonds and other interest rate-sensitive assets with growth-oriented credit assets in a single, dynamically managed portfolio.

This pairing helps diversify exposure to macro drivers and mitigate tail risks. Combining diversifying assets in a single portfolio makes it easier to manage the interplay of rate and credit risks and to readily tilt toward duration or credit according to changing market conditions.

Focus on quality credit. Since the conflict escalated, credit spreads have risen modestly off extremely tight levels but remain contained. Credit has repriced less sharply than stocks, and we expect that resilience to persist. We think yield levels are a more reliable guide to forward returns than spreads alone. And yields look compelling across many credit-sensitive sectors.

That said, the range of potential outcomes has widened, making selectivity key, in our view. Structural themes such as the AI-driven capex cycle continue to create opportunity, though we believe investors should approach them with cautious optimism, looking past extremes of enthusiasm and aversion. Recent geopolitical developments are also introducing additional uncertainty and dispersion across industries.

We think it makes sense to underweight cyclical industries, CCC-rated corporates - which account for the bulk of defaults - and lower-rated securitized debt, as these are most vulnerable. Mixing higher-yielding sectors across the rating spectrum - including high-yield corporates, emerging market debt and securitized assets - provides further diversification.

Temper equity volatility with high yield. Historically, high yield bonds have delivered returns comparable to equities but with meaningfully less volatility - and have generally outperformed equities in periods of below-trend growth. In our view, that makes high yield a credible complement for investors aiming to ease equity volatility without materially sacrificing return potential.

Harness a systematic approach. Today’s environment also increases potential alpha from security selection. Active systematic fixed-income approaches may help investors harvest these opportunities. Systematic strategies rely on a range of predictive factors that aren’t efficiently captured through traditional investing. Because systematic approaches depend on different performance drivers, we believe their returns complement traditional active strategies.

Protect against inflation. We think investors should consider increasing their allocations to inflation strategies, given the risk of future surges in inflation and inflation’s corrosive effect.

Staying Disciplined in a Disrupted Landscape

Taken together, we believe these elements create a more resilient fixed-income foundation. In our view, diversified sources of duration, balanced rate and credit exposures, and ample liquidity provide a framework that can absorb uncertainty while remaining nimble enough to quickly capture fresh opportunities as they arise.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: EWP: Spain Steadies Itself After A March Correction, Low-Teens P/E
data.description:
A year ago, President Trump trotted out those now-infamous posterboards detailing extremely high reciprocal tariff rates. So-called “Liberation Day" from the White House Rose Garden sparked a global stock market selloff. Today, there are similar vibes—just swap out “trade war” for “Iran war.” Equities are off the highs, bond yields are up, and volatility has spiked.

Another parallel is relative weakness in overseas stocks. Europe has been hit hard. Spain, namely, is well off its early-year record high. Today, I see value in the bank-heavy, value-focused country ETF. I have a "Buy" rating on the iShares MSCI Spain ETF (EWP). I’ll provide color on the fundamentals, valuation, and technicals. Seasonality also looks strong as we head into the middle part of the year.

Spain Outperforming YoY

Europe P/E Dips to 14x

According to the issuer, EWP seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of Spanish equities. It provides exposure to large and mid-sized companies in Spain and is used to express a single country view.

EWP is a small ETF, with just $1.8 billion in assets under management as of April 1, 2026. Its annual expense ratio is moderate at 50 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is about a full percentage point above that of the S&P 500 at 2.23%.

Share-price momentum is rated pristinely by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system—an A+. I’ll point out later, however, that the technicals and absolute price strength have deteriorated significantly just in the past five weeks. And while EWP Risk Grade looks good, the portfolio is highly concentrated, with two sectors dominating the allocation.

Finally, on liquidity, EWP has mixed metrics. Average daily volume is north of 800,000 shares over the past 30 days, while the 30-day median bid/ask spread is moderate at 9 basis points. That’s high enough for me to encourage prospective investors to use limit orders, particularly around the market open.

Looking closer at the portfolio, the 2-star ETF by Morningstar plots along the very top row of the style box, indicating its high allocation to Spanish large caps. Just 9% of the fund is classified as growth, with a notable 22% in value. Large-cap blend is far and away the largest weighting.

In terms of the valuation, EWP’s price-to-earnings ratio is very low at just 13.2x as of the end of February. After a March drubbing, that multiple is likely even lower to kick off the second quarter. Pair the low-teens P/E with a long-term EPS growth rate of 10.2%, and the resulting PEG ratio is very compelling at below 1.4x.

EWP: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Zooming in on the sector breakdown, Financials commands a high 42% position. Utilities is another notable overweight compared to the power-generation sector’s S&P 500 position. Those two value-oriented areas make up almost two-thirds of EWP.

Unfortunately, Energy is less than 5%, so it has not helped the ETF (France (EWQ) has a higher Energy stake in Europe). On the growth end of the style spectrum, Technology is a mere 5.4% of the Spanish equity ETF. So, investors should expect alpha if we see global Information Technology stocks underperform.

EWP: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, EWP has tended to perform very well in April and May over the past 10 years of price data. This augurs for a rebound following a steep 5.1% March 2026 plunge.

EWP: Bullish April-May Trends

The Technical Take

With an attractive valuation and bullish calendar trends, EWP’s technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that shares plummeted 12% from the February high to the March low. A strong bounce just recently only brings the Spain ETF back to its flat 50-day moving average—there's still major work for the bulls to do.

The good news is that the long-term 200-day moving average is on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. The 200dma is also below the price, which is a positive.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. It printed a series of lower highs at the tail end of the 2025-2026 rally to record highs. That was bearish divergence, portending the late-Q1 decline. Today, EWP remains in a high-congestion zone (evidenced by the volume by price profile on the left side of the chart).

The takeaway? We may see EWP trade sideways for a while. A correction through price occurred in March, another one through time could unfold into mid-year. Support is near $49.

EWP: $49 Support, Rising 200dma, Uptrend Broken

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on EWP. Shares have corrected, but the technical situation is not an all-clear. But the valuation is compelling, particularly if we see a rebound in banks and Utilities shares.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Anthropic Tries To Contain Claude Code Instruction Leak
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Anthropic scrambles after Claude Code instructions leak exposes internal tools. (0:15) Tesla deliveries miss expectations. (1:04) Hiring plans jump sharply in March despite slower 2026 outlook. (1:55)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Out top story so far, Anthropic (ANTHRO) is working to contain fallout after accidentally revealing internal instructions used to direct its AI coding agent, Claude Code.

The Wall Street Journal reports that Anthropic initially issued a broad copyright takedown request to remove more than 8,000 copies of the raw instructions shared on GitHub, before narrowing the request to 96 posts after saying the first sweep reached more accounts than intended.

A spokesperson said the leak of “some internal source code” did not expose customer data or the core model weights — the mathematical parameters that power its AI models. However, it did reveal commercially sensitive techniques and tools used to enable Claude Code to function as a coding agent.

Those tools, known as a harness, allow users to control and direct the model. The leak effectively gives rivals and developers insight into how to replicate key features without reverse engineering.

Among active stocks, Tesla (TSLA) delivered about 358K vehicles in Q1, missing the 365K consensus estimate. Production totaled more than 408K vehicles. Model 3/Y deliveries came in near 342K, with other models accounting for roughly 16K.

Shares of Bed Bath & Beyond (BBBY) are rebounding after the company announced plans to acquire several assets, including The Container Store, which emerged from bankruptcy last year and is now creditor-owned.

Steel and aluminum names including Nucor (NUE), Cleveland-Cliffs (CLF), ArcelorMittal (MT), and Alcoa (AA) are under pressure after The Wall Street Journal reported that finished products containing imported steel and aluminum would face a 25% tariff.

The tariff would apply to the entire value of derivative products containing those metals, replacing the current structure that applies duties only to the value of the steel or aluminum component.

Looking to the economy, with the official March jobs report arriving on Good Friday — a market but not federal holiday — Challenger’s report showed an uptick in hiring plans.

Employers announced 32,826 planned hires in March, up 157% from February and 149% from a year earlier.

Still, announced hiring plans for 2026 total 50,887 through March, down 6% from the same period in 2025.

In other news of note, McDonald’s (MCD) is expanding its value offerings as it looks to win back budget-conscious consumers.

Beginning in April, the chain plans to roll out menu items priced at $3 or less, alongside a $4 breakfast deal and lunch and dinner meal bundles starting at $5.

Under-$3 breakfast options include the Sausage McMuffin, Sausage Biscuit, Sausage Burrito, Hash Browns, and medium McCafé Premium Roast Coffee. Lunch and dinner items at that price point include the McChicken, McDouble, 4-Piece McNuggets, small fries, and a medium soft drink.

The company is also bringing its Meal Deal back to breakfast.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, BofA’s equity team is flagging stocks that hedge funds are heavily shorting, suggesting they could be candidates for a short squeeze on positive fundamental news.

Those names include Moderna (MRNA), Super Micro Computer (SMCI), Best Buy (BBY), Skyworks Solutions (SWKS), and Coinbase (COIN).

Check out the full list here.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Quiet Turnaround In UnitedHealth
data.description:
Investment Thesis

UnitedHealth Group (UNH) is flat since my last coverage, but the underlying business is significantly improved. The past two months have seen the management articulate their AI strategy, committing $1.5 billion to save $1 billion in costs. They’ve accelerated their automation in claims, pharmacy, and revenue cycles.

This is not just efficiency but margin expansion. The operating cost ratio is targeting 12.8%, with AI processing millions of interactions daily at high resolution rates. Meanwhile, the integration of the Optum platform is resulting in increased client stickiness. The market is not seeing it. I believe the business is compounding efficiency and is ready for the next leg up.

$1.5 Billion AI Allocation Extracts $1 Billion to hit a 12.8% Operating Cost Ratio in 2026

In my view, AI integration and overhead cost extraction form an operating advantage for UnitedHealth as it allocates $1.5 billion toward AI platforms in 2026 to extract $1 billion in operating costs. Optum Insight and Optum Financial Services merge to process healthcare transactions. Optum Real executes point-of-care approvals and monetizes claims instantly, and this shift removes post-service reconciliation. Providers receive cash faster.

As a result, UnitedHealth decreases admin labor, and operating cost ratios decrease due to this automation. UnitedHealth targets a 12.8% operating cost ratio for 2026, as the 2025 operating cost ratio is at 13.3%. AI answers 3 million calls per day, and the system hits an 18-second answer speed and resolves inquiries at a 90% rate on the first contact. With that, advocates redirect focus to escalated cases.

Additionally, OptumRx removes reauthorization mandates for 180 drugs that is reducing prior authorizations by 10%, and this automation removes friction in the pharmacy supply chain. OptumRx implements cost-based reimbursement models and passes 100% of rebates to clients that helped win 800 client contracts, and it expands operating margins by 0.2 percentage points in 2026.

Moreover, I see Optum Health consolidating electronic medical records. With that, employed provider groups operate on one of three platforms, which is down from 18 platforms. This consolidation feeds data into AI models uniformly. As a result, value-based care cohorts hit medical costs 30% below market averages, and patient satisfaction scores reach 90. Optum Health margin targets forecast a 2026 expansion of 0.3 percentage points, and Optum Insight margin targets forecast an expansion of 0.9 percentage points.

Further, revenue cycle management software utilizes AI to decrease human labor. For instance, Crimson AI saves money for hospital clients and increases software sales for UnitedHealth, and the automation extracts overhead and accelerates cash conversion. UnitedHealth derived $19.7 billion in cash from operations in 2025, with the 2026 cash flow target of $18 billion. These cash flow funds dividends, share repurchases, and acquisitions amid ROCE at 12.91%.

I believe decreasing operating costs counteracts medical care ratio (MCR) increases. UnitedHealth expects an MCR of 88.8% for 2026, with automating functions protects the net income margin. The net income margin currently hit 2.69%, with operating margins at 4.24%. EBITDA margins are at 4.83%. UnitedHealth projects $17.75 in adjusted EPS for 2026 under topline projections of $439 billion. Notably, UnitedHealth already recorded 2025 revenues of $448 billion, but with 2026 revenue, UnitedHealthcare targets margin expansion of 0.4 percentage points.

With the insurance unit implementing AI to process claims and manage enrollment, UnitedHealth reduces workforce headcount and rationalizes real estate assets to lower overhead. The $2.5 billion restructuring charge in 2025 included severance and lease terminations, and with that, UnitedHealth exits loss-making contracts. Optum Health drops non-aligned preferred provider organization contracts, and risk membership drops by 15%, which removes ancillary service risk.

I see UnitedHealth scales its operations without adding personnel by removing human labor from admin tasks. AI systems learn/adapt from daily interactions and compound cost savings over time, and the $1.5 billion investment in 2026 supports product development. Optum Financial settles payments instantly and replaces legacy billing processes, so this software ecosystem binds payers and providers to UnitedHealth platforms. UnitedHealth sells revenue cycle products to outside hospitals. Here, 8 out of 10 hospitals utilize UnitedHealth software, and 8 out of 10 payers utilize UnitedHealth software. Therefore, AI upgrades increase the value of existing software contracts.

Further, UnitedHealth manages $83 billion in total debt, and it targets a 40% debt-to-capital ratio by 2026’s end. Stable cash flow from automated operations pays down this debt. As a result, regular maturities occurring in H1-2026 receive cash payments. UnitedHealth has capital for stock repurchases as capital deployment may remain active due to overhead cost reductions.

As dividend yield reaches 3.41%, it is supported by cash per share of $26.89. UnitedHealth offsets membership losses through admin savings, and it is lowering the cost to serve patients and providers that protects the income statement from funding cuts. An asset turnover ratio of 1.47x means to me that utilization of assets to derive sales. For 2026, OptumRx forecasts earnings growth expanding into the high single-digit to low double-digit range, and Optum Insight projects earnings growth above 4%. With that, OptumHealth targets operating earnings growth near 9%, while UnitedHealthcare targets operating earnings growth near 13%.

Lastly, the integration of Optum Real and Optum Financial Services unlocks value pools for payers. So, UnitedHealth intends to automate 70% to 90% of certain admin functions from start to finish, and this mass automation secures profitability amid the reimbursement environment.

Margin Squeeze vs. Machine Efficiency: Can AI Outrun Policy Headwinds?

The main risk I see for UNH is the widening gap between medical cost inflation and government reimbursement. In 2026, medical costs are expected to increase by ~10%, while the 2027 Medicare Advantage rate update results in a 0.09% increase. This has already resulted in a medical care ratio of 89.1% for 2025, while Q4 reached 92.4%.

Management has targeted 88.8% for 2026, but this is based on benefit cuts and footprint changes, which are already resulting in member losses. Medicare Advantage is expected to lose 1.3 to 1.4 million members as a result of these changes, while overall membership is expected to decline by as many as 2.8 million.

This is a result of prioritizing margins over growth but creates opportunities for competitors to gain market share. This is important, as two-thirds of revenue comes from government programs. In addition, OptumRx's rebate pass-through model hurts margins, while increases in Medicaid rates are still lagging cost inflation. ACA enrollment is also decreasing while margins are still only 1% as a result.

Lastly, regarding the valuation, UNH trades at ~15x 2026 EPS of $17.86 and ~13.5x 2027 EPS of $20.03, reflecting a slight discount to its historical premium multiple. On a revenue basis, the stock trades at ~0.5x forward sales, despite generating more than $440 billion in revenue annually, which again reflects the market's skepticism on margin sustainability.

The answer lies in the fact that while growth metrics look poor in the near term, that’s where the upside lies. As cost efficiencies start to take hold via the power of AI, even a modest re-rating to historical multiples will provide a nice upside. I think we’re witnessing a classic case of sentiment discounting execution. If UnitedHealth Group meets its efficiency targets, I believe the stock’s valuation represents a buying opportunity.

Takeaway

While UNH is not yet re-rating, the business is quietly getting better. AI-powered cost extraction is redefining margin improvement, not just cost savings. As automation continues to grow and Optum builds out its ecosystem, the quality of the earnings is getting better. Investors are worried about pressure, but they are missing the compounding model that can drive the next re-rating.
